coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent N-CQDs uniformly implanted into sponge-like PVDF-HFP piezoelectric film by facile two-step process for multifunctional pressure sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shunjian
Huang, Ping
Luo, Yongping
Liu, Lei
Zhong, Wei
Zhang, Yuanjun
Wang, Yongya
Li, Xianchang
Hu, Shiming
Xiao, Zonghu
coredata.dc:description: To meet the diversified demands for the booming intelligent systems, a multifunctional pressure sensor with two basic capabilities of ultraviolet excited fluorescence and force-electricity response has been proposed, and named as fluorescent pressure sensor (FPS). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are being rapidly developed as an reasonable photoluminescence materials. However, the CQDs were utilized in the solid-state film along with serious aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ). Here, we developed a two-step method instead of the typical one-step method to overcome the AIQ of the CQDs in the representative piezoelectric polymer. The sponge-like poly (vinylidene) fluoride-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP) film with high porosity formed based on nonsolvent-induced phase separation was selected as the host matrix of the guest nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). The impressive bright blue fluorescence with enhanced intensity derived from the N-CQDs is successfully implanted into the obtained N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film, attributing to two key factors: (i) the sponge-like matrix supported uniform distribution of the N-CQDs and (ii) the hydrogen bonding between the N-CQDs and the matrix chains. Meanwhile, the hybrid film used in the FPS still keeps a sensitivity of 92.7 mV N−1, thanks to the β piezoelectric phase formation in the matrix. Since the hybrid film based FPS has multiple features, such as ultraviolet excited fluorescence, force-electricity response, self-powered capability and flexibility, it will show a great potential in the intelligent systems used as the multifunctional sensor and energy harvester.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
PVDF-HFP piezoelectric film
Solid-state fluorescence
Two-step method
Fluorescent pressure sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Education Department of Jiangxi Province ( KJLD13100; GJJ212301; GJJ212309; GJJ212318 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province ( 20224BAB214023 ) and Research Project of Huzhou College ( RK65007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the two-step fabrication of the fluorescent N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film and its application in the multifunctional FPS.

Fluorescent characteristics of the N-CQDs solution and the N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film under 254 and 365 nm wavelength excitations: (a) digital photos; (b) PL emission spectra; (c) PL emission intensity; (d) LSCM images of the hybrid film obtained under ultraviolet excitation. These exist a horizontal correspondence among the digital photos in (a), the PL curves in (b) and the histograms in (c) with same color.

Morphological and crystal characteristics of the fluorescent N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film: (a), (b) and (c) SEM images; (d) and (e) TEM images; (f) HRTEM image; (g) FT-IR spectra; (h) XRD curve; (i) differential thermal analysis (DTA) curves. The inserts in (a) and (d) exhibit the size distribution of the pores and the N-CQDs in the hybrid film, respectively. The magnified images of (b) and (c) are respectively show the slender skeletons and the starfish-like skeleton joints in the hybrid film. (f) focuses on the graphitic core of the N-CQDs with a (002) lattice spacing of around 0.32 nm in the hybrid film.

Performances and applications of the fluorescent CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film based multifunctional FPS: (a) real-time output voltage under repeating different forces; (b) applied force-voltage curve; (c) digital photos of the device under 254 and 365 nm wavelength ultraviolet (UV); (d) detecting finger bending and showing a corresponding potential used as electronic skin for intelligent robot with multifunctions, including light perception, anti-counterfeiting and tactile perception; (e) capturing wind energy and exhibiting a corresponding potential applied as the energy harvester for integrated umbrella with bifunctions, including the protection of human body from ultraviolet burns and the production of green energy from the natural wind and the vibration of people walking.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent N-CQDs uniformly implanted into sponge-like PVDF-HFP piezoelectric film by facile two-step process for multifunctional pressure sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Huzhou College, Huzhou 313000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Huzhou College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Huzhou College, Huzhou, 313000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University, Xinyu 338004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinyu Institute of New Energy, Xinyu University, Xinyu, 338004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Xinyu University, Xinyu 338004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Xinyu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Xinyu University, Xinyu, 338004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To meet the diversified demands for the booming intelligent systems, a multifunctional pressure sensor with two basic capabilities of ultraviolet excited fluorescence and force-electricity response has been proposed, and named as fluorescent pressure sensor (FPS). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are being rapidly developed as an reasonable photoluminescence materials. However, the CQDs were utilized in the solid-state film along with serious aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ). Here, we developed a two-step method instead of the typical one-step method to overcome the AIQ of the CQDs in the representative piezoelectric polymer. The sponge-like poly (vinylidene) fluoride-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP) film with high porosity formed based on nonsolvent-induced phase separation was selected as the host matrix of the guest nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). The impressive bright blue fluorescence with enhanced intensity derived from the N-CQDs is successfully implanted into the obtained N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film, attributing to two key factors: (i) the sponge-like matrix supported uniform distribution of the N-CQDs and (ii) the hydrogen bonding between the N-CQDs and the matrix chains. Meanwhile, the hybrid film used in the FPS still keeps a sensitivity of 92.7 mV N−1, thanks to the β piezoelectric phase formation in the matrix. Since the hybrid film based FPS has multiple features, such as ultraviolet excited fluorescence, force-electricity response, self-powered capability and flexibility, it will show a great potential in the intelligent systems used as the multifunctional sensor and energy harvester.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:sections.ce:para: With the rapid development of intelligent systems in daily life, pressure sensors endowed with self-powered capability, flexibility, multifunctionality and high resolution have gradually become a hot topic [1]. Based on the sensing mechanisms, the main pressure sensors are divided into piezoelectric, piezoresistive, capacitive and triboelectric, etc [2]. Among them, the piezoelectric effect can endow the device with a self-powered capability. Further, when this self-powered pressure sensor employing the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (including derivatives PVDF-HFP and PVDF-TrFE, etc) with β phase as a pressure sensitive possesses an another character of flexibility. Therefore, the PVDF based sensor could be widely applied in sensing fields, such as electronic skin, intelligent wearable and human-computer interaction, and energy conversion field as nanogenerator. Additionally, to promote the multifunction of the device, a sensor named as the fluorescent pressure sensors (FPS) with two basic abilities of ultraviolet excited fluorescence and force-electricity response is proposed in our previous work [3]. However, the proposed FPS cannot work without external power supply, due to employing the piezoresistive hybrid film composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and mesoporous TiO2 matrix as the sensitive layer. Recently, the fluorescence function was incorporated into the PVDF piezoelectric film by using the MWCNTs and Eu complexes as the fluorescent fillers to achieve a self-powered FPS [4].

As an important photoluminescence (PL) material, the CQDs are being rapidly developed for many applications, including fluorescent labeling, anti-counterfeiting and biological sensing, because of their excellent PL performance, biocompatibility and facile process [5–7]. In general, the CQDs exhibit favorable multicolor fluorescence in solution, owing to their abundant surface functional groups induced high dispersion in solution [8]. By comparison, when the CQDs utilized in the solid-state materials, a very serious aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) is happened, depending on the preparation process and matrix environment [9,10]. One-step method employing a mixture of the CQDs and matrix materials is typical channel to introduce the CQDs into the solid-state matrix, such as polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), PVDF, agarose gel and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [10–12]. However, the one-step technique is difficult to effectively avoid the AIQ, especially facing new matrices with different chemical environments [10]. Referentially, when the CQDs are used as the sensitizers in the quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSCs), the CQDs with single-particle level are uniformly adsorbed on the surface of mesoporous TiO2 skeletons by using a facile two-step procedure, and the produced cells show preferable stability due to the strong chemically anchoring between the CQDs and the skeletons [13,14]. In this process, the mesoporous TiO2 film is first deposited on conductive substrate, and then followed by adsorbing the CQDs on the TiO2 film.

Inspired by those impressive works, we attempted to develop the two-step method instead of the typical one-step method to overcome the AIQ of the CQDs in representative piezoelectric polymer. The sponge-like PVDF-HFP film with high porosity formed based on nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS), which plays a similar role to the mesoporous TiO2 film in the QDSCs, was selected as the host matrix of the guest nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). In brief, PVDF-HFP was first dissolved in a mixed solvent of acetone and dimethyl formamide (DMF), and then spin-coated on ITO-PEN electrode to finish the step one. In the step two, the N-CQDs solution was immediately spin-coated on the deposited PVDF-HFP layer. After the solvent volatilization at room temperature and subsequent heating treatment at 120 ℃, the fluorescent N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film was obtained. The preparation process of the hybrid film is shown in Fig. 1.

The guest N-CQDs was synthesized using cotton fiber as carbon source with ethanol amine as dopant by hydrothermal method [3]. The light absorption of the N-CQDs mainly concentrates in ultraviolet region with two obvious peaks at the wavelengths of 288 and 350 nm (Fig. S1), which are respectively related to the π→π * transition of CC segment and the n→π * transition of CO bond [8,15]. Consisting with the ultraviolet absorption characteristic of the N-CQDs, the maximum excitation of the N-CQDs is happened at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S2). Therefore, when illuminated under 365 nm ultraviolet, the N-CQDs solution is changed to light blue with high brightness from light yellow, as shown in Fig. 2(a). This fascinating fluorescence of the N-CQDs is successfully inherited to the solid-state N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film obtained based on the developed two-step process. When the irradiation wavelength declines to 254 nm, both of the samples including the N-CQDs solution and the hybrid film show dark blue with low brightness. The above phenomena are further verified by a combination of a Smart Fluo-QY PL spectrometer and a Zeiss LSM710 laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). From the PL emission spectra and intensity in Fig. 2(b) and (c), the N-CQDs in the PVDF-HFP matrix has a identically similar curve profile with those in the solution under the same excitation condition. An excitation-dependent emission peak is occurred at blue region. With the excitation wavelength increases from 254 to 365 nm, the emission peak take places an obvious blue shift from 437 to 427 nm and from 443 to 427 nm respectively for the N-CQDs in the solution and in the PVDF-HFP matrix, and meanwhile the emission intensity sharply reinforces. The emission intensity of the N-CQDs in the hybrid film is approximately 1–2 orders of magnitude higher than that of the N-CQDs in the solution. From the LSCM images in Fig. 2(d), an uniform distribution of the N-CQDs in the PVDF-HFP matrix is observed, resulting in the blue fluorescence emission of the hybrid film transferred from the N-CQDs under ultraviolet excitation.

The impressive bright blue fluorescence with strengthened intensity emitted in the N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film first thanks to the unique sponge-like structure of the matrix supported uniform distribution of the N-CQDs. The matrix has the well-developed pores with a size of 1–6 µm, the starfish-like skeleton joints with similar size to the pores, and the slender skeletons with nanoscale, which are revealed by a combination of a Zeiss EVO MA10 scanning electron microscopy (SEM), a Shimadzu SPM-9600 atomic force microscopy (AFM) and a Cewei LW300LJT optical microscope (OM), as shown in Fig. 3(a)-(c), S3 and S4. Further, the disperse N-CQDs in the hybrid film is also affirmed by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) apart from being identified by the LSCM in Fig. 2(d), as displayed in Fig. 3(d) and (e). Hence, relative to the 2–5 nm sized N-CQDs, such sponge-like structure of the PVDF-HFP matrix can provide an effective isolation support for the N-CQDs, which extends the distances between the luminescent centers and further avoids the Foster energy transfer and the AIQ [16]. Especially, the starfish-like skeleton joints are beneficial to support enough N-CQDs to ensure the fluorescence intensity. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 3(f) reveals that the N-CQDs in the hybrid film keep a graphitic core with a (002) lattice spacing of around 0.32 nm. The hydrogen bonding between the N-CQDs and the PVDF-HFP chains is then appreciated for contributing the bright blue fluorescence emitted in the hybrid film. The abundant surface groups on the hydrothermal N-CQDs (Fig. S5), such as hydroxy (OH), carboxyl (COOH) and amino (NH2), could effectively form hydrogen bonds with the PVDF-HFP chains, resulting in high fluorescence quantum yields through the limit of the intramolecular motions and the inhibition of the nonradiative relaxation [17–19]. The Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectra in Fig. 3(g) obtained by a Thermo Nicolet5700 spectrometer confirm the formation of the hydrogen bonds. With the N-CQDs composited in the PVDF-HFP matrix, the absorption band at 3418 cm−1 becomes stronger and slightly shifts to the lower frequency of 3425 cm−1, suggesting that the O-H bond is stretched by the interaction between the hydroxyl on N-CQDs and the PVDF-HFP chains [20]. Last, the contact interface between the N-CQDs and the ITO-PEN electrode blocked by the PVDF-HFP thin layer plays a positive role in the fluorescence emission of the hybrid film [10].

The piezoelectric properties of the fluorescent N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film used in a simple sandwich-like FPS were tested. For configuring the device, another ITO-PEN electrode was physically attached on the hybrid film. The output voltages of the FPS by simply undergoing repeating different forces are shown in Fig. 4(a) and Fig. S6, and the corresponding applied force-voltage curve is demonstrated in Fig. 4(b). Obviously, there exists a excellent linear relation with R2 > 0.999 between the voltage and the force for the sensor with a sensitivity (S=ΔV/ΔF) of 92.7 mV N-1. The sensitivity of PVDF-HFP film without N-CQDs is 73.8 mV N-1, which is obviously lower than the film with N-CQDs (92.7 mV N-1), because of the induced polarization of CQDs embedded in the PVDF-HFP matrix, as shown in Fig. S7. Moreover, when the time of the repeated loading test extends to 140 s, the output voltage of the device always keeps stabilization (Fig. S8). The reproducibility and durability of the FPS are also tested, and the voltage output signals of the FPS at different time (≦30 d) under the applied force of 1 kgf (10 N) are recorded as Fig. S9. It can be found that the voltage signal of multiple devices prepared by the same process has little change under the same force in the long application process, which can prove that the device has good repeatability and durability. In addition, the fluorescence of does not change significantly after being used for one month, as shown in inset of Fig. S9. The piezoelectric effect of the hybrid film is mainly determined by β phase with a TTT conformation in the PVDF-HFP matrix [21]. When a Bruker ADVANCE D8 X-ray diffraction (XRD) is taken, an obvious broad peak at 2θ = 20.24° is observed, as displayed in Fig. 3(h), belongs to the sum of the diffraction at (110) and (200) planes of the β phase in the hybrid film [22]. In the two-step process, the polarization effects induced by the polarity of DMF solvent and the ITO-PEN substrate can help the β phase formation in the hybrid film [20]. Meanwhile, the dispersed N-CQDs may act as the induced active sites for the β phase nucleation through the hydrogen bonding between the N-CQDs and the PVDF dipoles (CH2 and CF2) [18]. In addition, the heating treatment at 120 °C over the curie temperature (T c) employed for the hybrid film is beneficial to the β phase formation. When the hybrid film begins to cool from above the T c, the α phase with a TGTG conformation will be transformed to the β phase [23]. A NETZSCH STA2500 thermogravimetry reveals that the T c of the hybrid film is 105 °C, while the melting temperature (T m) of the hybrid film is about 140 °C, as shown in Fig. 3(i).

The comprehensive analyses of the fluorescent N-CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film indicate that the developed hybrid film possesses two basic functions of ultraviolet excited fluorescence and force-electricity response, which can endow the FPS with multiple features, including ultraviolet excited fluorescence, force-electricity response, self-powered capability and flexibility. As presented in Fig. 4(c), under illumination with a wavelength of 365 nm, the impressive bright blue fluorescence emitted in the hybrid film is kept in the device, though the dark blue fluorescence emitted in the ITO-PEN causes an interference to a certain degree. It also means that the ITO-PEN is not a reasonable electrode for the FPS. The potential application of the multifunctional FPS was assessed and looked forward in two scenarios. The first one is attaching the flexibility device on the finger joints to detect finger bending in an attempt to be applied for the electronic skin and the human-computer interaction. As exhibited in Fig. 4(d), the voltage of the FPS climbs with increasing bending angle. Conversely, depending on the voltage generated by the FPS, the angle of joint bending can be obtained. Therefore, when the multifunctional FPS used as the electronic skin, several new functions of light perception, fluorescent self-alarming and anti-counterfeiting except for tactile perception will be given for the intelligent robot. The second one is the device used to capture wind information and energy. As shown in Fig. 4(e), a pair of positive and negative voltage signals is generated by blowing a simulated wind from the hair dryer through the FPS. The electrical signals disappear when the simulated wind stops. Hence, when the multifunctional FPS is integrated on an umbrella as a ultraviolet absorbed energy harvester, this FPS based umbrella may not only protect human body from ultraviolet burns, but also produce green energy from the natural wind and the vibration of people walking.

In summary, the bright blue fluorescence with strengthened intensity derived from the N-CQDs was successfully implanted into the PVDF-HFP matrix through a facile two-step method to prepare a novel fluorescent CQDs/PVDF-HFP piezoelectric hybrid film. The overcome of the AIQ of the N-CQDs in the PVDF-HFP matrix is mainly attributed to the sponge-like matrix supported disperse N-CQDs and the hydrogen bonding between the N-CQDs and the matrix chains, resulting in high fluorescence quantum yields of the hybrid film. The force-electricity response thanks to the β piezoelectric phase formed in the PVDF-HFP matrix, which is mainly related to the synergistic effect of the induced polarization from the substrate, solvent polarity and N-CQDs particles as well as the heating treatment. As a result, the hybrid film used in the FPS still keeps an acceptable piezoelectric capability, with an S of 92.7 mV N−1 and R 2 > 0.999. Since the hybrid film based FPS has multiple features, including ultraviolet excited fluorescence, force-electricity response, self-powered capability and flexibility, it will show a great potential applied in the intelligent systems as the multifunctional sensor and energy harvester.

ce:section.ce:para: Shunjian Xu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Ping Huang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Yongping Luo: Supervision. Lei Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei Zhong: Investigation, Visualization. Yuanjun Zhang: Formal analysis. Yongya Wang: Investigation. Xianchang Li: Validation. Shiming Hu: Validation. Zonghu Xiao: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Education Department of Jiangxi Province (KJLD13100; GJJ212301; GJJ212309; GJJ212318), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20224BAB214023) and Research Project of Huzhou College (RK65007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131232.
